titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Edward Cahill SJ to Fr Thomas V Nolan SJ, 11 December 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Edward Cahill

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from Fr Edward Cahill SJ (1868-1941) to Fr Thomas V Nolan SJ (1867-1941), 11 December 1915, in which Fr Cahill outlines the invitation that he gave the Irish Volunteers to drill at Mungret College. Cahill explains that he is trying to establish a cadet corps among the pupils of Mungret College (like Fr William Hackett's corps at Sacred Heart College, Crescent, Limerick). Cahill invited both the Irish Volunteers and National Volunteers to drill at Mungret and informs Fr Cahill that Fr Corboy SJ is misinforming him about the Irish Volunteers, as his cousin is the principal man in the National Volunteers.
Fr Edward Cahill SJ (1868–1941) was born at Callow, Ballingrane, Co. Limerick. He joined the Society of Jesus (10 November 1890) and was ordained priest in 1897. From then until 1923 he was at Mungret College, Limerick as master, prefect of studies, and rector, and as superior of the apostolic school attached to the secondary school. In 1936, Cahill sent Éamon de Valera, with whom he was very friendly, submissions outlining catholic principles on which he believed the new constitution being drawn up by the head of government ought to be based.

correspAction.placeName: St Francis Xavier's, Upper Gardiner Street, Dublin

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Mungret College
Limerick
Dec 11 1915 Very Rev and dear Fr Provincial
P C Your letter dated December 9th reached me
only this afternoon when I was going to class. Its contents surprised me very much;
and I hasten to reply. The whole matter has been most misrepresented to you; and
I fear rather grossly so. When you speak of the Sinn Fein Volunteers you mean I
presume the Irish Volunteers. Sinn Fein is a misnomer put on them by the Politicians
in Limerick at the least the name is most misleading. Their executive committee includes
besides at least one priest some of the very very first citizens in position and ability such as U. O Callaghan (city
Tannery) O Toole ( city Treasurer etc The history of their invitation to Mungret is this: - I have been most anxious
for the past two years to get up some kind of a Cadet Corps amongst the
boys with a view to teach drill which several patents ask for. Last year
I established paid an instructor £20 for 1/2 hour 1/2drill every day with the boys
of the different divisions; but the whole thing proved a dismal failure. This year
several of community suggested to form a Cadet Corps which the boys would
enter voluntarily and manage themselves under the supervision of a scholastic someth something like Fr Hackett
Corps in Limerick which seems to be a great success. I had talked this over with sev several and had resolved to do it; and in order
to encourage the boys to take an interest in it I told Fr Corboy (who was very keen on
Mungret College
Limerick
it that I had asked ) Hackett to bring out his corps here some Sunday. Fr Corboy
however objected (as Fr Hacketts boys were too small to make any impression on our
lads) and suggested to ask out the LimerickVolunteers — his cousin Mr Corboy is the principal
man in the National Volunteers & he offered to arrange the matter with him. I discussed the
matter with all the other consultors and finally determined with the approval of all
(except Fr Corboy whom I did not consult any further on the matter as I found it too difficult
difficult to so so) to ask out Each sections of the Volunteers to come here on the same
day and give a display to the boys & the consultors ( vr F Byrne F. Casey and
F. Tomkin) were of opinion that we could not ask one section without the asking
the other as otherwise our action would mark the identification of the college
with that party: but that n no one n could or de facto would object
to our asking both; e —especially th as in Limerick there is no real opposition
Both have marched in the same procession more than once: salute each other and seem likely to form the same body
invery soon.
InI accordingly interviewed the leadleaders of both Volunteer Sections more
than two weeks ago as to the possibility of both coming here together next Sunday They both the Irish V
They both promised to lay matter before their committees and let mme know. The the
National Volunteers however e implying that they would much prefer to that
the Irish Volunteers would come at have a different time. The next II heard was a
letter from Mr Corboy last Su Sunday morning that the Nat Volunteers
were coming that very day. They came ab about after midday drilled
in playground: and the boys enjoyed it. I told them publicly why I had
asked them. A very full report of the proceedings were published a day or two
later in the Freeman in the Limerick papers and evidently political capital was being being made
of it to identify us with that party. Last Wednesday the Irish Volun-
teers wrote enquiring is next Sunday I would suit us for their visit. I
again talked the mmatter over with Fr Minister Fr Casey Fr Tomkin. I found
it useless to discuss such matters with Fr Corboy as he does not understand yet
understand the house; and I find his vie views to very one sided and his accounts
of facts very unreliable. They were unam unanimously of opinion
that it was now quite essential to have the Irish Volunteers who are the
more m numerous in Limerick of the two and have apparently the sympathy of
the Bishop the priests and of of most except the the Unionists & proffprofessional
politicians and are as a body the best best men and best Catholics in Limerick.
I accordingly wr wrote accepting their their visit on Sunday. Since then I have
written again and have interviewed the leader arranging details & have written I have al also spoke to all the
boys at my religious knowledge classes telling them of the coming visit of the Volunteers &
of its object & that they may see good drill & that we our corps would not be
be affiliated with or identified with any party. F Corboy interviewed me
about the matter a few days ago saying that the boys were opposed it to them
I made enquiries about that and found it to
be quite without foundation. They The boy, outside a & very few few know little
or nothing about politics and practically all the boys in the house are very Irish
in their sympathies and will neither boys or parents to not no not I know It I think for certain have any
opinion hostile to the Irish Volunteers. This is also the opinion of all the other
Consultors. I read your letter today and t talked the matter over with the
the same three consultors th separately this evening. All Three were strongly of
opinion that your order was given under a mis misapprehension and as a
result of false or misleading information and that it the
carrying out of it litterally would inevitably have the very effect which you
wish to avoid. & feel that it would not have been givn given
if you you knew the facts as we know them here. I myself am
of the same opinion. Besides it would be quite impossible t now to
cancel arrangements without causing a sent sensation and a scandal
both in college and outside and giving vey very grave offence to many of the best people in Limerick
and such action would be likely to have serious effects for the college, Hence
although I see the matter my may be serserious for myself I have no alternative
but to let things go on. It is quite certain that t as far as boys and
outsiders are concerned the whole thing visit will be forgotten in a week whereas
a to co cancel the such an invitation now would inflict rouse feelings
that probably never wo would be forgotten.
And now that I have laid the whole matter before you as fully as I can
in a hurried letter I think I have the right to ask that you investigate
the matter and learn if what I say is accurate and prevent if possible
such unfair and misleading information bebeing sent in future.
Asking a you prayers I remain
and respectfully
Ed Cahill SJ
